sections.heading: MongoDB Search Features
sections.body:
The following MongoDB Search features are not available with on-prem deployments:

- Backup and Restore

- File Copy-based Initial Sync (FCIS)

- Query Tracking

- Encryption

> **Note:** You can manually implement Backup and Restore or BYOK Encryption.

sections.heading: mongot TLS Configuration Limitations
sections.body: `mongot` has the following configuration limitations regarding TLS.

sections.heading: Cipher Suites
sections.body:
MongoDB has a predefined list of cipher suites that are recommended for use with
HTTP/2 with Netty and only uses suites in that list. Accordingly, you cannot
specify a cipher suite for your on-prem deployment.

sections.heading: FIPS
sections.body:
`mongot` does not support FIPS mode, regardless of support and configuration
in `mongod` or `mongos`. If you enable FIPS mode on `mongod` or
`mongos`, MongoDB does not enable FIPS mode on `mongot` and may result in
undefined behavior. For more information on MongoDB and FIPS, see
[Configure MongoDB for FIPS](manual/tutorial/configure-fips).

sections.heading: Host Name Validation
sections.body:
The `mongot` use of the gRPC listen server does not support TLS/SSL hostname
validation, inclluding checking the Subject Alternative Name (SAN).

sections.heading: Supported Clients
sections.body: To learn about supported clients, see [index](fts-about-indexing) and [Queries](ref-query-management).

sections.heading: Index Limitations
sections.body:
If you create a MongoDB Search index that has or will soon
have more than 2.1 billion index objects, you must use `numPartitions`
or [Deploy a Sharded Cluster](create-cluster-sharding). For this limit, each top-level
document or nested `embeddedDocument` in the indexed collection
fields counts as one object

By default, MongoDB Search stops replicating changes for a single index that grows larger
than 2.1 billion index objects on any given replica set member or shard.
This means your index remains queryable, but you might
get stale results.

If your collection contains documents that are 16MB or larger, MongoDB Search fails to index your
document, causing your index to become  [STALE](index-statuses), and initiates a full index rebuild.
You must delete the offending document(s) from your collection in order for index rebuild
to successfully complete. This issue can also occur when update operations on large
documents cause the change stream event to exceed the 16MB BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) limit. To avoid this, we
recommend that no single document in your collection exceeds 8MB. Consider the following
best practices:

- Structure your documents to minimize the size of sub-documents or arrays.

- Avoid operations that update or replace large fields, sub-documents, or arrays.

To learn more, see [Change Streams Production Recommendations](manual/administration/change-streams-production-recommendations) and
[Reduce the Size of Large Documents](reduce-document-size-anti-pattern).

To learn more about index limitations, see:

- [Manage MongoDB Search Indexes](ref-index-limitations)

- [Multi Analyzer Limitations](ref-multi-limitations)

- [Data Type Limitations](bson-data-types-limitations)

sections.heading: Other Limitations
sections.body:
If you're using a [clustered collection](manual/core/clustered-collections)
and have the [notablescan](manual/reference/parameters) parameter set to
`true`, your MongoDB Search indexes may not finish building. To resolve this issue, you
must set the `notablescan` parameter to `false` or check your log for index
status transitions.
